name: Handle 'Send Me an Email' as a Deflection
description: When a prospect responds to a cold or follow-up call with 'just send me an email', distinguish between (a) a legitimate channel preference, (b) a polite brush-off, and (c) a buying signal in disguise — and respond appropriately to each, without forcing the call.
disclaimer: Skills assume the call is solicited by the operator. NEVER use these to power a mass-cold-call campaign without the operator's explicit per-prospect authorisation.

context.when_to_use: On any outbound call where the prospect, before substantive conversation has happened, asks the agent to send information by email. The agent must read the situation in seconds and pick a response.
preconditions: Agent has gotten past the gatekeeper and is speaking with the target (or their delegate).
Agent has a real, sendable artefact appropriate for this prospect — not a generic deck.
Operator has been briefed on the agent's policy for email follow-up (always comply / push once / never comply on cold).

principles: 'Send me an email' is information, not an answer. Read the tone, the speed, and the context before deciding.
Comply gracefully when in doubt. Refusing to send email when asked is a worse signal than sending and following up.
Push back ONLY when you have a clear, concrete reason a 30-second live exchange is more valuable for THEM than email — and you can say that reason out loud.
Never argue. The moment you sound like you're not accepting their stated preference, you lose.
An email follow-up is a real commitment. If you say you'll send something, send something real, within the window you named.

phrases.comply_clean: Sure — what's the best email for you? I'll keep it short, one or two paragraphs and a link, not a deck-bomb.
phrases.qualify_before_sending: Happy to. Quick question first so I send the right thing rather than a generic email — is the [topic] something you're actively looking at, or more of a 'down the road' thing?
phrases.single_push: Sure, I can do that. Honestly though — there's one thing I'd rather just ask you live for 30 seconds, then I'll send the rest as written follow-up. Is now OK, or worse timing than I think?
phrases.respect_the_no: No problem — what's the best email? I won't follow up beyond the one note unless you tell me to.
phrases.honest_about_brushoff: Totally cool if 'send me an email' is your nice way of saying not interested — would you tell me straight, just so I don't keep your inbox warm for no reason?
phrases.concrete_commitment: I'll send it within the hour today. Subject line will say [X] so it's easy to find or ignore.

tactics.name: Read the request in three seconds — preference, brush-off, or buying signal
tactics.script: Preference (calm, neutral tone, after some engagement) → comply cleanly. Brush-off (fast, before any real exchange, slightly cool tone) → respect the no AND offer one honest out. Buying signal ('send me a proposal') → comply with qualification first.

tactics.name: Comply cleanly when the request is genuine
tactics.when: Tone reads as legitimate channel preference.
tactics.script: Use `comply_clean`. Some prospects genuinely do their best work async. Forcing live time on them is a tax they'll resent.

tactics.name: Qualify briefly before sending if the email would benefit
tactics.when: Prospect seems engaged enough that 60 seconds of context would meaningfully change what you send.
tactics.script: Use `qualify_before_sending`. One question — not three — to avoid sending a generic deck.

tactics.name: Push back exactly once if there's a real reason
tactics.when: You have a specific live-only question whose answer reshapes the email.
tactics.script: Use `single_push`. If they decline, drop it immediately — do not push twice on the same call.

tactics.name: Surface the brush-off if you suspect one
tactics.when: Request was fast, before any real exchange, and follow-up emails are likely to vanish into ghosting.
tactics.script: Use `honest_about_brushoff`. This surprises most prospects and produces honest answers — they'll either say 'no, actually, send it' or 'yeah, not for us', and either saves time.

tactics.name: Make the commitment concrete on the call
tactics.script: Use `concrete_commitment`. Name a window, name a subject line. Vague 'I'll send something across' rarely gets opened.

boundaries: Do NOT pretend a follow-up email requires live information you could easily figure out from public sources.
Do NOT push back more than once. Two attempts to keep them on the phone after they asked for email is pressure.
Do NOT promise an email and not send it. Better to not commit than to break the small promise.
Do NOT send a generic deck. Send something written FOR this prospect, even if 80% is templated.
Do NOT cc the operator or the prospect's boss on a follow-up email unless explicitly authorised.
success_signals: Prospect gives their preferred email and a topic to focus on.
Prospect responds to the follow-up email within a reasonable window.
Prospect agrees to a small live exchange when the push-back was honest and well-framed.
Prospect admits it's a no — clean signal, time saved.
failure_signals: Prospect repeats 'just email me' more crisply (push again and you've lost them).
Prospect goes silent on the email follow-up for 10+ business days.
Prospect's stated email bounces or is a generic shared inbox — they didn't want this to land.

exit_strategy.on_success: Send the promised email within the named window. Keep it under 8 lines. One link, one ask, one calendar option. End the call warmly with a thank-you.
exit_strategy.on_failure: If the brush-off was confirmed, log the prospect as 'declined — do not retry by phone'. If they went silent post-email, follow the ghosted-lead re-engagement cadence — not a re-call.
follow_ups: Send the email by the committed deadline, with the committed subject line.
Single written nudge 5-7 business days later if no response — then stop.
Log outcome in CRM with the prospect's exact words about preferred channel.

required_user_info: Operator's standing policy on email-deflection responses
Real artefacts the operator wants the agent to send (not 'a deck' — actual links/files)
Operator's preferred follow-up cadence after the email lands
Whether the agent can quote pricing or capabilities in the written follow-up
Operator's outbound email signature / sending identity
